data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-06T01:38:31.533Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET/EF Core migration. Let me provide what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression of changes around synchronous API support:

1. The Cosmos DB provider has historically only supported async operations natively (since the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only).

2. In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), synchronous operations on the Cosmos provider started throwing by default, but there was an opt-in to allow sync-over-async behavior.


3. In EF Core 9, you could configure the provider to permit synchronous operations through configuration options, though this was discouraged. Moving forward to .NET 10 and .NET 11, the trend appears to be stricter enforcement of async-only patterns, potentially removing or deprecating the sync-over-async workaround entirely.

Let me reconsider the actual progression more carefully. In EF Core 9, synchronous calls on Cosmos throw an exception by default, but there's an `EnableSyncOverAsync()` configuration method to opt in. The question is whether this escape hatch persists in later versions or gets removed to force full async adoption. The Cosmos provider in EF Core only supports async operations natively since the underlying SDK is async-only, so sync calls were always implemented as sync-over-async wrappers. In .NET 10 this was available as an opt-in, but .NET 11 removes that option entirely and throws `InvalidOperationException` on sync calls, forcing developers to convert everything to async equivalents.
